coredata.dc:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose/gelatin film incorporated with eggplant peel waste-derived carbon dots for active fruit packaging applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Ajahar
Riahi, Zohreh
Kim, Jun Tae
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were derived using eggplant peel by a hydrothermal approach and incorporated into the carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and gelatin (Gel) blend to develop sustainable and functional packaging films for fruit preservation. The CD was uniformly dispersed within the CMC/Gel blend to form a dense and continuous film and fashioned a strong interaction with the polymer chain, increasing the tensile strength of the film by 5.0–16.0 %. Also, with the impregnation of CDs, the UV-blocking potential of the CMC/Gel film was greatly improved to the extent of blocking 94.3 % of UV-B and 72.5 % of UV-A, while the water vapor permeability slightly decreased (by 2.7–5.4 %), and the water contact angle of the film marginally expand (by 6.2–19.1 %). The CMC/Gel film with 3 wt% of CD added depicted strong antioxidant efficacy of 100 % against ABTS and 59.1 % against DPPH and displayed strong antibacterial action that inhibited the progress of Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli by 99.8 %. In addition, when table grapes were packaged using a CMC/Gel composite film containing CD and stored at 4 °C for 24 days, the fruits packed with the composite film maintained excellent external quality and extended the shelf life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CMC/gelatin film
Carbon dots
Active packaging
Fruit preservation
Food safety
Extension of shelf life

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Development of CMC/gel based packaging film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422) and the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (2022H1D3A2A02092788).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphic representation for the synthesis of eggplant peel-derived CDs and the development of CMC/Gel-based composite film.
(a) TEM image, (b) lattice fringe, (c) histogram for distribution of size, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e) UV–vis spectra for excitation and emission, and (f) fluorescent emission response of the EP-CDs.

Functional activities of EP-CDs: (a-d) antibacterial activity using well diffusion assay, (e-h) antifungal activity using food poisoning technique, and (i) antioxidant activity using ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging assays.

(a) FTIR response of CMC/Gel, CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2, and CMC/Gel@CD3 films. (b) Broad range XPS survey and peak fitted high-resolution spectra for (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, and (f) S2p of CMC/Gel, CMC/Gel@CD3 films.
(a-c) Surface and cross-section view analyzed by recording FE-SEM images, (d) real digital images, (e) light transmittance response, and (f) UV-blocking aptitude of the CMC/Gel-based films.

(a, b) Antioxidant efficacy of the CMC/Gel-based films for the ABTS and DPPH assays. The antibacterial effect of the CMC/Gel-based films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes was investigated by (c, d) total viable colony count method. (e, f) Bactericidal effect of the fabricated films.
(a) Actual view of table grapes packaged with the CMC/Gel-based films for 24 days at 4 °C. (b) pH, (c) firmness, (d) weight loss rate, and (e) TVC value of packaged table grapes for 24 days at 4°C.
Color values and optical properties of the CMC/Gel-based packaging films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as means ± SD. Any two means in the same column followed by the same letter are not significantly (p < 0.05) different from Duncan's multiple range tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: WVP, WCA, and mechanical attributes of the CMC/Gel-based films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Findings are presented as means ± SD. According to Duncan's multiple range tests, values in the same column that have the same superscript letter do not differ substantially (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose/gelatin film incorporated with eggplant peel waste-derived carbon dots for active fruit packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were derived using eggplant peel by a hydrothermal approach and incorporated into the carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and gelatin (Gel) blend to develop sustainable and functional packaging films for fruit preservation. The CD was uniformly dispersed within the CMC/Gel blend to form a dense and continuous film and fashioned a strong interaction with the polymer chain, increasing the tensile strength of the film by 5.0–16.0 %. Also, with the impregnation of CDs, the UV-blocking potential of the CMC/Gel film was greatly improved to the extent of blocking 94.3 % of UV-B and 72.5 % of UV-A, while the water vapor permeability slightly decreased (by 2.7–5.4 %), and the water contact angle of the film marginally expand (by 6.2–19.1 %). The CMC/Gel film with 3 wt% of CD added depicted strong antioxidant efficacy of 100 % against ABTS and 59.1 % against DPPH and displayed strong antibacterial action that inhibited the progress of Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli by 99.8 %. In addition, when table grapes were packaged using a CMC/Gel composite film containing CD and stored at 4 °C for 24 days, the fruits packed with the composite film maintained excellent external quality and extended the shelf life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: After harvesting, large quantities of vegetables and fruits decompose due to rot and decay, wasting huge amounts of food resources and causing huge financial losses every year [1]. Meanwhile, the adulteration of fruits and vegetables destroys their dietary content and promotes pathogenic microbes that pose a sincere risk to human health [2]. Among various fruits, table grapes (Vitis vinifera L.), one of the most productive and popular economic fruits worldwide due to their excellent taste, texture and nutritional value, contain secondary metabolites such as phenolics and vitamins [3]. They also have significant economic value in the global fruit industry due to their heavy use in products such as raisins, juice, wine and other edible staples [4]. However, due to the juicy pulp texture and thin cuticle, nectarines are prone to spoilage. Additionally, microbial invasion, chemical contamination, external forces, and moisture loss that occur during postharvest storage, transportation, and retailing affect the texture, color, and appearance of the fruit, reducing the market worth of the fruit [2,5].

Accordingly, in order to safeguard the quality and safety of fruit, we utilize food packaging technology to minimize quality loss and resulting damage during storage and transportation [6]. However, the food industry faces several limitations due to the packaging methods currently in use. For example, fruit is susceptible to cold damage during refrigerated storage if the storage temperature falls below 13 °C [7]. Nonetheless, these problems can be mitigated through radiation, biological control agents, chemical disinfectants, or controlled/modified environments. However, disinfectant residues can pose risks to the environment and public health. Second, radiation and regulatory environments require expensive equipment. Future developments in the fruit industry are therefore focused on sustainable and environmentally friendly technologies to sustain packaging eminence in the fresh fruit supply chain.

Much research has been conducted to determine the most effective way to package a variety of fruits, including grapes, lychees, strawberries, bananas, peaches, blueberries, cherries, and pomegranates [8]. However, because the influence of packaging materials on microbial development varies depending on the kind of food and storage surroundings, microbiological safety cannot be ensured on its own [9]. Therefore, modified active packaging appears to be the best way to preserve fresh fruit, as it can provide antioxidant and antibacterial properties in addition to UV protection properties inside the package throughout the supply chain.

The serious ecological problems brought about by the overuse of petroleum-based, non-biodegradable plastic packaging materials can be overcome by adopting biodegradable polymers, including proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids due to their biodegradability and environmentally friendliness [2,10]. For example, proteins like gelatin (Gel) are affluent in functional sites (-COO/-COOH-, -OH, and -NH2), which can easily interact with various active materials to realize sustainable packaging systems [11]. The formation of electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds can expand the mechanical strength and biological activity of packaging films [10]. However, Gel-based films have the problem of high hydrophilicity and low physical strength [12]. In order to improve the physical characteristics of Gel films, mixing polysaccharides with Gel to produce films can expand the tensile strength and other physicochemical properties of single biopolymer films [10]. Among the polysaccharides that can be used for this purpose, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a biodegradable polysaccharide polymer commonly used for food safety prospects because of its profuse natural resources, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and renewable potential [6,13]. The carboxyl groups of CMC provide anions that can form charge interactions and improve the strength of the binary polymer matrix.

However, CMC/Gel film does not have the bactericidal, radical scavenging, and UV shield properties necessary to maintain the quality and ensure the safety of packaged foods. Typically, antibacterial, antioxidant and UV shielding properties of packaging materials are achieved by incorporating bioactive functional substances. In contrast, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) are generally recognized as sustainable due to their excellent chemical stability, biocompatibility, surface functionalization, and low toxicity. CDs are attractive antibacterial/antioxidant agents with high potential for active and intelligent food packaging [14]. The excellent electron acceptor and electron donor functions of CDs (<10 nm) account for their antioxidant properties, which are attributed to reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation photothermal and photoelectric effects [15]. Additionally, CDs have abundant π-π conjugated groups at their core, providing excellent UV absorption performance [14]. Owing to the high specific surface area, CDs can be easily impregnated into biopolymer matrices and release active ingredients more effectively, reducing the number of active ingredients needed to maintain fruit quality while minimizing adverse impacts on the sensory characteristics of the fruit [16].

By the way, eggplants are one of the most common vegetables grown worldwide, with global production reaching 52.3 million tons in 2016 [17]. The eggplant skin is completely edible. However, larger or older ones have a bitter taste and should be peeled. The peel generated during eggplant processing has no commercial value but contains a large amount of phenolic components and polysaccharides. Suppose CDs are manufactured using eggplant peel, a by-product generated during eggplant processing, and used as a multi-functional food packaging additive. In that case, food waste can be recycled and eco-friendly and sustainable resource use can be expected. This strategy could not only provide the necessary means to recycle food waste but also expand the focus of ongoing research into packaging and advance the creation of new hybrid materials with intended functional characteristics.

Therefore, the main aim of this work was to apply eggplant peel-derived carbon dots (EP-CDs) to engineer CMC/Gel-based functional films for the applications of active food packaging, especially to safeguard quality and prolong the shelf life of fruits.

CMC (sodium salt of carboxymethyl cellulose) (with 250,000 of average MW and 0.9 degrees of substitution:) and gelatin (Fish gelatin, 200 Bloom, Type A, Food-grade) were received from Junsei Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan) and Gel-Tec Co., Ltd. (Seoul, Korea), respectively. Agar powder, tryptic soy broth (TSB) and BHI (brain heart infusion broth) were procured from Duksan Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Ansan, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 23235, Escherichia coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895, Aspergillus flavus, Penicillium chrysogenum, Salmonella enterica, and Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 were obtained from the KCTC (Korean Collection for Type Culture, Seoul, Korea). 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and potassium persulfate were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Fresh table grapes were purchased from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea).

Eggplant peel (EP)-derived carbon dots (CDs) were obtained using the hydrothermal carbonization strategy, as described in a previous report, and some modifications were made [18]. Briefly, waste EP was thoroughly washed with distilled water and then dried in an air oven at 50 °C and crushed well using a commercial mixer grinder to obtain a fine powder. Then, 1.0 g of EP powder was dispersed in distilled water (50 mL) in a Teflon-lined reactor (100 mL) with mechanical stirring for 15 min and transferred into a muffle furnace to heat at 190 °C for 7 h (Fig. 1 ). After cool down to 25 °C, the carbonized clear supernatant of EP was obtained by centrifuging at 12,298 ×g (10,000 rpm) for 20 min. The supernatant solution was sieved through a nylon filter (pore size: 0.22 μm, diameter: 25 mm, Whatman membrane filter), and the obtained yellow-brown EP-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of CMC/gel based packaging film
ce:section.ce:para: CMC/Gel-based packaging films were engineered via a solution-casting procedure [19]. For this, 2 g of each CMC and Gel were dissolved in separate beakers in distilled water (100 mL) by stirring vigorously at 70 °C for 3 h, and the mixing of both polymer solutions was carried out in a 1:1 ratio with constant stirring at 50 °C till to attain a homogenous blend solution. After that, glycerol (1.2 g, i.e., 30 wt% based on polymer) was added into the solution mixtures, and various amounts of the EP-CDs (0, 1, 2, and 3 wt% based on CMC/Gel) were added to the CMC/Gel solution while stirring vigorously for 60 min. The obtained solutions (200 mL) were cast onto a Teflon film-coated glass plate (30 × 24 cm2) and kept for drying at 25 °C (Fig. 1). Additionally, the control film was prepared for comparison utilizing the same process without CD addition. The desiccated films were collected and conditioned for 48 h in a model FX 1077 humidity chamber (Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., Ansan, Korea) controlled with RH of 50 % and 25 °C. The prepared films were designated as CMC/Gel, CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2 and CMC/Gel@CD3, according to the concentration of EP-CDs.

The morphology of obtained CDs was evaluated using TEM (transmission electron microscopy), and their optical parameters were evaluated using fluorescence and UV–vis spectrophotometry. Film morphology was observed using FE-SEM (field emission scanning electron microscopy), and their chemical and crystalline characteristics were assessed using ATR-FT-IR (attenuated total reflectance, Fourier transforms infrared) and XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) analysis. Additionally, film characteristics such as optical, tensile, water vapor barrier, water contact angle, and functional properties, including antioxidant and antibacterial properties, were determined using the techniques detailed in the Supporting Information.

Table grapes were used to evaluate the efficacy of CMC/Gel@CD films in protecting food quality and freshness and expanding shelf life. Briefly, CMC/Gel-based film pouches (10 cm × 10 cm) were prepared using a heat sealer, 40 g of table grapes were packaged, sealed with polyethylene (PE) wrap, and then kept for 24 days at 4 °C. Three replicate samples were selected from every group at predetermined time intervals (0, 8, 16, and 24 days) to test the quality of the packaged fruits. In this regard, the color characteristics and pH changes of the stored fruit were determined. The gravimetric technique was employed to govern the total weight loss of grapes during storage. The TVC (total viable colony count) approach was employed to guess the invasion of food pathogens in stored samples. For this, 5 g of grape samples were homogenized for 180 s in 45 mL of peptone water (0.1 %) using a Stocker (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France). The agar plates were then inoculated using 1 mL of homogeneous aliquots, i.e., diluted serially. Following 48 h of incubation at 37 °C, colonies were enumerated, and data from findings were given as Log CFU/g.

Film properties were measured with individually prepared film samples as the three replicated experiments, and the results were presented as mean ± SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significance of each mean value was determined (p < 0.05) by Duncan's multiple range tests using the SPSS statistical analysis software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The shape and size distribution of the produced EP-CD were analyzed using TEM images. The synthesized EP-CDs had approximately quasi-spherical morphology (size varying from ∼2.4 to 6.3 nm) and were partially monodisperse due to electrostatic repulsion between anionic charges (-OH and -COO- groups) on the surface (Fig. 2a). As shown in Fig. 2b, spacing between the organized and parallel lattice fringes of EP-CDs was approximately ∼0.21 nm, which is closely related to the diffraction plane (001) for graphite [20], confirming the successful synthesis of CDs from eggplant peel [21]. Additionally, the size distribution histogram of EP-CDs plotted with randomly selected particles from TEM images showed that the CDs' average diameter was around 4.1 nm (Fig. 2c).

By examining FTIR spectra, surface functional groups of EP-CDs can be recognized to understand their properties better. Fig. 2d confirms that EP-CD exhibits many hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) amine (-NH) on the surface, which is indicated by the wide peak around 3424 cm−1, which is expected to have a hydrophilic nature of CD [21]. The vibrational absorption at 2958 and 2825 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of different types of CH groups, and the stretching vibrations at 1562 cm−1 and 1666 cm−1 coordinated with the amide II (CN vibration stretching and NH bending vibration) and CO groups, respectively [22]. The CC (1616 cm−1), CN (1489 cm−1), and CN (1403 cm−1) bonds stretching vibrations were also noticed in Fig. 2d, indicating that during the carbonization reaction, the polyaromatic structures emerged in EP-CDs [23]. Furthermore, O=C-OH and CO stretch were represented by the vibration peaks at 1404 and 1200 cm−1, respectively [23].

The optical aspects of the EP-CDs were further assessed by the UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission response (Fig. 2e,f). As depicted in Fig. 2e, the UV–vis spectrum response of the EP-CDs exhibited maximum absorption at 277 nm due to transitions (π-π*) of CC bonds and a minor lump at 320 nm, which mainly corresponded to transitions (n-π*) of CO and CN. These peaks confirm that CDs can absorb ultraviolet radiation [21,24]. The addition of these CDs to packaging films is expected to protect food from deterioration caused by environmental UV radiation. The inset image of EP-CD aqueous solution emits a blue color when exposed to ultraviolet light at 365 nm due to the characteristic phenomenon of CD [22]. The wavelengths of maximal excitation and emission for CDs were observed to be approximately 369 and 443 nm, respectively (Fig. 2e).

Photoluminescence (PL) investigation of the synthesized EP-CDs (Fig. 2f) with the varying excitation wavelengths revealed that the wide blue emission intensity possessed the peak of maximum strength (λmax ∼ 443 nm) when exposed to 370 nm of excitation wavelength. A pronounced bathochromic change from 409 nm to 536 nm in fluorescence peak was tracked during the change in the excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 500 nm. The varying dispersion of particle sizes and resulting lower energy paths for emission were revealed by the red shift of the fluorescence peak [18].

The antibacterial potential of EP-CDs was evaluated employing the agar well diffusion method (Fig. 3a-d), which showed that EP-CDs had strong antibacterial action against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. The inhibition zones of EP-CDs against E. coli, S. enterica, S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes were 3.1, 4.8, 5.6, and 7.1 mm, respectively. The results also showed that the EP-CDs have more potent antibacterial action toward Gram-positive than Gram-negative strains. The cell walls of Gram-positive bacteria have a thick, single peptidoglycan layer that facilitates interaction with CDs, allowing CDs to enter the cell nucleus more directly. On the other hand, interaction with CDs is more difficult for Gram-negative bacteria due to the thin peptidoglycan layer with the outer cell wall [14].

Additionally, a food poisoning technique was adopted to demonstrate the antifungal activity of EP-CD dots against P. chrysogenum and A. flavus, and the outcomes are revealed in Fig. 3e-h [25]. The EP-CDs act as a poison to molds (hence the term “food poisoning technology”) and successfully inhibit mold growth, showing significant antifungal activity against P. chrysogenum and A. flavus fungal strains. After 5 days of incubation, the control group showed that fungal growth for both A. flavus and P. chrysogenum strains totally covered the surface area of the Petri dish. However, in the EP-CD treatment group, the growth of P. chrysogenum and A. flavus was found to be noticeably reduced (Fig. 3f,h). CD has been reported to enhance antifungal activity against unicellular and multicellular fungi [26,27].

Regarding the antibacterial mechanism of action due to the abundance of hydroxyl, amide and carboxyl moieties on the surface, EP-CD binds to the pathogen cell surface and transfers oxygen molecules to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [6,24]. ROS such as singlet molecular oxygen (1O2), superoxide (O2 •−), hydroxyl free radicals (OH•), and hydroperoxyl (HOO•) radicals disrupt the oxidative and antioxidant equilibrium, sequentially destroy biomolecules present in the microbial cell wall, leading to cell lysis [6,24]. By oxidizing polyunsaturated phospholipids in cell membranes, it can efficiently prevent the growth of bacteria and fungi through ROS formation. EP-CD has thus shown its potential to eliminate harmful and perishable bacteria and expand the possibilities of creating interesting hybrid active food packaging materials.

CDs can scavenge free radicals because they have a high potential to donate electrons or hydrogen (H•). Therefore, determining the antioxidant potential is essential for the production of effective antioxidants for active food packaging [28]. The antioxidant action of the synthesized EP-CD was investigated based on free radical scavenging of ABTS and DPPH. The results of the antioxidant assay are listed in terms of inhibition rate, as shown in Fig. 3i. The EP-CD was observed to exhibit an increasing trend in ABTS and DPPH scavenging actions as CD concentration varied from 25 to 100 μg/mL. Afterward, when the concentration of antioxidant (EP-CD) reaches 100 μg/mL, ABTS and DPPH antioxidant efficacy reaches 100 % neutralization level. At the same time, the dark purple color of the DPPH solution shifted to a yellowish hue, while the dark blue color of ABTS changed to a colorless state in the presence of EP-CD, indicating that CD can remove the radical moiety.

In addition, the essential concentration of EP-CDs to scavenge 50 % of the initial ABTS and DPPH radicals was measured to determine the average effective scavenger concentration (EC50). Then, based on the average effective scavenger, the AAI (antioxidant activity index) of the EP-CDs in the ABTS assay was 27.1 ± 0.5 %, while that of the DPPH assay was 8.0 ± 0.1 %. In general, an AAI of <0.5 is supposed to have weak antioxidant efficacy, an AAI of 0.5 to 1.0 is suggested to have moderate antioxidant efficacy, an AAI of 1.0 to 2.0 is suggested to have strong antioxidant action, and an AAI >2.0 is considered to have very strong antioxidant activity [29].

The radical scavenging potential of EP-CDs corresponded to the abundance of oxygenated surface functional groups of the CDs. Mechanistically, it has been widely discussed that upon antioxidant generation, DPPH• and ABTS•+ radicals can combine and quench through the transfer of hydrogen (H•) or electrons from the -OH of CD to form stable DPPH-H and ABTS-H, which leads to changes in solution appearance [30].

The FTIR observation of CMC/Gel, CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2, and CMC/Gel@CD3 films are depicted in Fig. 4a. The wide absorption bands corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the OH group confirmed by the absorption peak appeared at 3280cm−1. The IR bands at 2934, 2878, and 1031cm−1 indicated the stretching of CH, -CH2, and C-O-C bonds' antisymmetric tensile vibration, respectively [31]. The peaks noticed at 1153,1555, and 1634 cm−1 are credited to NH stretching vibration (amide III), NH (amide II) bending vibration, and CO (amide I) stretching vibration [32]. However, the additional peak around 1740 cm−1 was due to the -C=O that appeared in the IR spectra of CMC/Gel loaded with various concentrations of EP-CDs. In addition, Fig. 4a confirmed that major characteristic IR peaks of CMC and Gel were retained in CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2, and CMC/Gel@CD3 films. Ascribed to the H-bond between CMC/Gel and EP-CDs, the peak of amide I (3280cm−1) in the neat CMC/Gel film was slightly shifted to 3291cm−1 in the CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2, and CMC/Gel@CD3 films [24]. In the IR observation of CDs containing CMC/Gel films, the characteristic peaks of all films were observed to be almost similar except for slight differences. The reason for the identical IR absorptions was the similar chemical environment of these bands, which retained the same structural morphology propelled via H-bonding among the functional moieties of gelatin (carboxylic, amide, hydroxyl), CMC (carboxylate, hydroxyl), and EP-CDs (carboxylic, hydroxyl, amide).

The XPS survey scan of CMC/Gel and CMC/Gel@CD3 films showed three typical characteristic elements such as C (283.5 eV), N (298.6 eV) and O (530.3 eV) conforming to the C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 4b, Table S1, Supporting Information). An additional peak of S (166.8 eV) corresponding to S2p appeared in the CD-containing film, suggesting that the surface functional groups of CD are involved in the construction of the complex with the CMC/Gel film (Table S1). Fig. 4c shows that C 1 s XPS spectra of CMC/Gel and CMC/Gel@CD3 films exhibited C=C/C − C, CN, CO and O-C=O groups assigned to the binding energies of 284.1, 285.5, 286.7, and 287.9.3 eV, respectively [23]. Fig. 4d shows that the N1s XPS response of both films could be split into three main CN, CN and NH bonds indicated by the peaks at 398.2, 398.8, and 399.4 eV of binding energies, respectively. After the incorporation of CDs, the NH bonds' maximal strength increased., confirming that the atom of N from the EP-CDs was present in the CMC/Gel@CD3 matrix. Further, the absorptions of O=C-O, C-O-H/C-O-C and O=C − O − C bonds were observed in the deconvoluted spectra of O1s corresponded to the absorptions at 530.3, 531.5, and 532.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 4e) [33]. In addition, the S2p spectrum exhibited three peaks (Fig. 4f) at 164.8, 166.6 and 167.8 eV, which were due to the C-S–O, -OSO3 2− (S2p3/2), and -OSO3 2− (S2p1/2), respectively [21]. Studies of the chemical composition showed that the CD surface contains many functional groups that promote the establishment of strong H-bonds with the CMC and gelatin matrix, resulting in more stable films.

FE-SEM analysis was accomplished to realize insight into the potential impact of EP-CD on the morphological properties of CMC/Gel-based films. FE-SEM micrographs of the film surface and cross-section are represented in Fig. 5a-c. All film samples had consistent and homogeneous surfaces, signifying that EP-CD was effectively distributed into the polymer blend. The neat film (CMC/Gel) has a uniform exhibits a cohesive internal structure with smooth surface appearance (Fig. 5a). The compact structure and homogeneous dispersion of CDs in CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2, and CMC/Gel@CD3 films indicate that EP-CDs are evenly distributed inside the binary polymer matrix. Overall, high homogeneity was observed across all film surfaces and cross-sections (Fig. 5b,c), indicating that the resulting CMC, gelatin, and EP-CD had high interaction and compatibility.

The elemental composition of the CMC/Gel and CMC/Gel@CD3 films' surface was determined using energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) analysis, and the findings are shown in Fig. S1.a-c (Supporting Information). Elemental mapping shows that the main characteristic elements of the CMC/Gel matrix, such as carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, were found to be evenly distributed across the film surface. On the other hand, an additional element (sulfur) in the CMC/Gel@CD3 films was uniformly dispersed, representing that the CDs were dispersed equally throughout the polymer matrix. Three characteristic elements of CMC/Gel (i.e., oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon) were also identified through EDS spectra. For CMC/Gel@CD3 film, additional sulfur peaks with higher nitrogen element concentration appear, signifying the occurrence of EP-CD in the polymer matrix.

Food packaging applications are highly reliant on the physical and optical characteristics of the film, including color, transparency, and UV-blocking ability. The real digital images and UV–vis survey response of the CMC/Gel-based films are exposed in Fig. 5 d-f. The CMC/Gel film was transparent, shiny, and colorless (Fig. 5d). In contrast, the films containing EP-CDs were tinted related to the control film. With increasing EP-CD concentration, the color changed from colorless to yellowish. This color change, resulting in yellow pigmentation, was caused by the presence of brown-yellow EP-CD [34]. Overall, the clarity of the CMC/Gel@CD3 film was well maintained even with the addition of 3 % EP-CD.

The consequence of the integration of EP-CDs on the apparent color of fabricated films was quantitatively evaluated using color L, a, b, ΔE (total color difference), WI (whiteness index), and YI (yellowness index), and the results are shown in Table 1 . The CMC/Gel@CD1, CMC/Gel@CD2, and CMC/Gel@CD3 films exhibited substantial variances (p < 0.05) compared to the CMC/Gel in the L, a, and b values. With the addition of yellowish EP-CD, the a value of CMC/Gel films declined, while the b-value increased from 1.4 to 18.6. Due to the sharp increase in the b-value, the YI of CMC/Gel@CD3 film amplified to 30.9 compared to 2.2 (CMC/Gel film). Overall, the integration of EP-CD marginally lessened the brightness of the CMC/Gel film, and the WI value decreased noticeably, while the YI and ΔE values significantly increased.

The UV–Vis transmittance response of the films is exposed in Fig. 5e. The UV light barrier characteristics and transparency of the CMC/Gel-based films at 280 nm and 660 nm, respectively, are listed in Table 1. The transparency (T660) of the CMC/Gel film was 87.1 % and slightly decreased to 85.1 % by the addition of EP-CDs, indicating that the film made of CMC/Gel is very transparent and the addition of EP-CDs did not considerably decrease its transparency [34]. On the other hand, the T280 (UV-barrier property) of the CMC/Gel film was 31.9 %, having a moderate UV-blocking property attributable to the gelatin's UV barrier effect [34]. However, the addition of EP-CDs reduced the T280 significantly, showing excellent UV-blocking properties. When 3 wt% of EP-CDs were added, the film blocked the T280 by >99 %. In particular, it blocked ∼72.5 ± 1.3 % of UV-A and 94.3 ± 2.1 % of UV-B (Fig. 5f). The excellent UV-blocking properties of the composite films are attributed to the UV-absorbing ability of EP-CD, which has a high content of polyphenolic materials rich in aromatic groups whose abundant π-π conjugated bonds block UV irradiation [14,35]. It is believed that the CMC/Gel@CD3 film has the potential to block UV radiation effectively, guaranteeing food safety and increasing packaged products' shelf life [36].

The water vapor permeability of the control (CMC/Gel) and CMC/Gel@CDs films are shown in Table 2 . It is interesting to note that the WVP of the CMC/Gel film lessened pointedly (p < 0.05) despite the integration of hydrophilic EP-CDs [21]. The electrostatic repulsion amid the protonated -NH2 moieties of CMC/Gel and EP-CD can promote water transfer due to the pores created by the molecular distance [37]. However, there was no substantial difference (p > 0.05) in the WVP between composite films with varying concentrations of EP-CDs, which may be due to their compatibility with the polymer matrix, forming a dense and smooth microstructure, as evidenced in FE-SEM analysis (Fig. 5a) [21]. The WVP of CMC/Gel films was similar to that of previously published CMC (1.07–1.66 × 10−10 g m−1 s−1 Pa−1) and gelatin film with garden cress' extracts (3.23–4.72 × 10−11 g m−1 s−1 Pa−1) [38,39].

The WCA of the film was estimated as an indicator to appraise the wettability of the produced film and determine the hydrophilic or hydrophobic characteristics of the film [19], and the findings are revealed in Table 2. The WCA of the CMC/Gel film was 66o, suggesting that its surface is hydrophilic, but it enhanced significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of EP-CDs. The WCA of the proposed films increased may be because of the H-bonding amid the active sites (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide moieties) of EP-CDs with the film matrix, resulting in a uniform and dense structure [40]. Compared to the control (CMC/Gel) film, the surface functional moieties of CDs incorporated within the composite film and the active sites of the CMC/Gel polymer matrix created H-bond connections, which improved the hydrophobicity of the film [19].

The mechanical characteristics of films play an imperative role in their application as suitable materials in the packaging food industry. Results for the mechanical parameters of the fabricated films in terms of elongation at break (EB), tensile strength (TS), and elastic modulus (EM) are presented in Table 2 and Fig. S2. The TS (71.4 MPa), EB (12.9 %), and EM (1.8 GPa) of the control CMC/Gel film indicate that it has a strong, rigid, and stiff nature. Incorporating various concentrations of EP-CD showed a significant increase (p < 0.05) of 15.9 % for TS and 70.5 % for EM of the composite films, while there was a notable decrease of 41.1 % for EB. This increase in TS is caused by hydrogen bonds between the polymer matrix and various functional groups of EP-CD. As shown in the FE-SEM results, EP-CDs with large surface areas can be uniformly dispersed in the CMC/Gel matrix to enhance TS by increasing the interfacial interaction and improving the load distribution. Instead, as EP-CD was integrated, the flexibility of the film (EB) decreased. In general, the flexibility of a film is inversely proportional to the strength of the film (TS) due to the addition of nanofillers [42,43].

Functional characteristics such as antioxidant and antibacterial activities in food packaging films are key features for active packaging applications [41,42]. The antioxidant and antibacterial efficacy of the CMC/Gel films incorporated with various concentrations of EP-CDs were assessed, and the results are revealed in Fig. 6 .

The ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging efficacy of the CMC/Gel-based films was determined, and the findings are presented in Fig. 6a,b. The neat CMC/Gel film showed slight radical scavenging action against the DPPH and ABTS radicals with neutralizing 15.4 % and 30.5 % radicals, respectively, which is due to the antioxidant activity of gelatin [43]. Instead, the antioxidant activity of the CMC/Gel film increased significantly with the integration of the EP-CDs, and the increase in the antioxidant efficacy of the film was dependent on the concentration of the EP-CDs [41]. Of course, the antioxidant efficacy of the composite film is ascribed to the strong radical scavenging ability of EP-CDs (Fig. 3i). Due to the ability of CD to donate H atoms, DPPH• and ABTS•+ radicals are quenched by the transfer of hydrogen (H•) or electron from -OH of the CD to form stable DPPH-H and ABTS-H [14]. Therefore, the antioxidant potential of the film against DPPH and ABTS was remarkably enhanced when EP-CD was incorporated into the CMC/Gel matrix. The radical scavenging action of CMC/Gel-based films increased with increasing weight of the CD-loaded film, with CMC/Gel@CD3 film showing the highest ABTS (100 %) and DPPH (59.2 %) radical scavenging activities.

The antibacterial activity of the CMC/Gel-based films with different concentrations of EP-CDs was investigated against Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (L. monocytogenes) using colony count assays, and the findings are shown in Fig. 6c-f. The results (Fig. 6c,d) clearly demonstrate the antibacterial effect of the produced composite film through growth inhibition. The CD-added films showed distinct growth inhibition compared to the neat CMC/Gel film, and the antibacterial activity of the films increased with the CD concentration. The antibacterial action of the composite films was relatively low at low CD concentrations (Fig. 6 e,f). However, the bactericidal action of the film with 3 wt% EP-CDs (CMC/Gel@CD3) exhibited 99.8 % inhibition of E. coli and L. monocytogenes growth after 12 h of incubation. Of course, the antibacterial activity of the composite film is due to the antibacterial function of EP-CDs. The bacterial cell membrane is composed of a layer of phospholipid molecules and amphipathic peptidoglycan. The dynamic habitability of CDs in biofilms is influenced by their high specific surface area and nanoscale size, which influence bacterial growth and division [33]. Additionally, some CDs are taken up by bacteria and affect genetic processes by altering the secondary structure of the genetic material using non-covalent bonds [44]. The CD-added CMC/Gel films, which have high antibacterial properties, are expected to be used in antibacterial active packaging applications.

Grapes are perishable fruits with high physiological activity and a short shelf life [45]. Therefore, by using an appropriate packaging method, the quality of grapes can be maintained, and the shelf life can be extended, thereby improving the commercial value of grapes. Grapes were packaged with CMC/Gel-based film and stored at 4 °C for 24 days to evaluate changes in quality, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . When stored at 4 °C for 24 days, all packaged grapes showed changes in appearance. Unpackaged, polyethylene (PE) film-wrapped, and CMC/Gel film-treated groups showed significant changes, while CMC/Gel@CD film-treated groups showed minimal changes (Fig. 7a). The appearance of mildew spots mainly characterized these changes, which might be attributable to enzymatic browning and microbial invasion of the fruits. It is worth noting that the change in appearance of unwrapped grapes was less compared to fruit packaged with PE and neat CMC/Gel films, whereas the quality degradation of fruit packaged with EP-CD-containing film was relatively minor. In particular, fruits packaged with CMC/Gel@CD3 film maintained excellent visual quality throughout the storage period. These results indicate that selecting an appropriate packaging film is important. These changes in the appearance of grapes are related to quality attributes such as pH, firmness, weight, and total viable colonies (TVC) (Fig. 7b-e).

Quality characteristics such as pH, firmness, weight loss rate, and TVC are essential indicators to evaluate the quality characteristics of fresh fruits after harvest and storage [46]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the pH of grapes increased with increasing storage time in all treatments. However, grapes packaged with CMC/Gel@CD3 film showed the least change in pH during storage. Enzyme activity and fruit ripening can increase the pH of grapes during storage and distribution, ultimately reducing acid content. Typically, the pH of fresh table grapes ranges from 3.3 to 4.2, but fruit type, storage conditions, microbial contamination, and degree of ripeness can affect pH changes [45]. The results of pH change during storage showed that packaging with CMC/Gel@CD3 film could maintain the pH of grapes during storage and maintain quality even after 24 days of storage.

Firmness changes of the packaged grapes during storage are shown in Fig. 7c. It is known that the decrease in fruit firmness during storage is caused by fruit aging, depolymerization or pectin hydrolysis, and cellular disintegration and softening [47]. As the storage period increased, the firmness of the grapes gradually decreased. The decrease in firmness of grapes showed a significant difference depending on the packaging method. The unpackaged, PE film packaged, and CMC/Gel film treated groups showed a rapid decrease in firmness, while the CMC/Gel@CD film treated group had a relatively slow rate of decrease. In particular, the CMC/Gel@CD3 film treatment group showed the slowest decrease in firmness and was found to be the best in maintaining the firmness quality of grapes.

The weight loss of table grapes steadily increased during the storage period. The weight loss rate of the control groups was fast, but the weight loss proceeded more slowly in the CD-added film packaging groups, especially in the CMC/Gel@CD2 and CMC/Gel@CD3 film treatment groups (Fig. 7d). After 24 days of storage, the weight loss rate of grapes in the control group was 19.5 %, while the weight loss rate of grapes packaged with CMC/Gel@CD2 film and CMC/Gel@CD3 film was 7.6 % and 4.1 %, respectively, decreased by 2.6 to 4.6 times the weight loss. Therefore, the use of CMC/Gel@CD3 film can greatly suppress the quality degradation of table grapes during storage. The antioxidant potential of CMC/Gel@CD3 film can reduce the damage of reactive oxygen species to fruit cells and improve the antioxidant capacity of fruit, thereby preserving the aesthetic value of packaged foods.

The initial TVC of packaged grapes was ∼1.7 Log CFU/g but increased rapidly during storage, reaching ∼4.9, 6.5, and ∼ 7.0 Log CFU/g in controls (no packaging, PE, and CMC/Gel film), respectively, after 24 days of storage (Fig. 7e). On the other hand, bacterial growth was noticeably delayed in grapes packaged with CMC/Gel@CD2 and CMC/Gel@CD3 films. The TVC of grapes packaged in CMC/Gel@CD3 film was less than ∼2.5 Log CFU/g. The antibacterial properties of CMC/Gel@CD3 film can counteract the rapid increase in respiration rate caused by adversity stress and reduce microbial invasion into table grapes, slowing down weight loss while maintaining pH and hardness. The results confirmed that CMC/Gel@CD3 film could be used as an active packaging material to extend the shelf life of fruits after harvest.
Table S3 also presents the efficiency of the CMC/Gel@CD film compared to other published CMC/Gel-based active packaging films.

In this study, eggplant peel-derived CDs were used as functional fillers to improve the physicochemical properties of CMC/Gel-based films and prepare active packaging films with antibacterial and antioxidant potential. It was found that kitchen wastes (eggplant peels) were a more economical and environmentally safe source for CD production. CMC/Gel film with added CDs not only has increased physical and chemical properties but also exhibits excellent functional properties such as antioxidant, antibacterial, and UV-blocking properties. When applied to preserve and store table grapes after harvest, this functional film effectively suppressed contamination of foodborne pathogens, maintaining the aesthetic characteristics and quality of grapes stored at 4 °C for 24 days, thereby extending the shelf life. Therefore, it is expected that CDs can be manufactured using food waste or food processing by-products, such as eggplant peels, and used as functional fillers to manufacture eco-friendly functional packaging materials and use them as active packaging materials.

Ajahar Khan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zohreh Riahi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
